sections.heading: Traditional use fell, and the decline was mainly about withdrawal
sections.body: Among all women aged 15 to 49, traditional contraceptive use declined from 9.8 percent in 2022 to 8.1 percent in 2025. Withdrawal made up most of traditional use in both years, falling from 8.1 percent to 6.8 percent. Calendar or rhythm use was much smaller, declining from 1.7 percent to 1.3 percent. Other traditional methods were recorded at 0.0 percent in both years, so the national change is almost entirely a story about withdrawal and, to a lesser extent, calendar or rhythm.

sections.heading: The highest-use regions form a cluster, not a single outlier
sections.body: Traditional contraceptive use was highest in Eastern Visayas at 12.1 percent, followed by Ilocos Region at 11.8 percent and Western Visayas at 10.8 percent. Central Luzon followed at 10.1 percent, while CALABARZON and Bicol Region were both at 9.7 percent. This upper group is spread across Luzon and the Visayas, so high traditional use is not confined to one island group or one urban-rural profile. Withdrawal accounts for most of the level in the two highest regions, reaching 10.9 percent in Eastern Visayas and 10.8 percent in Ilocos Region.

sections.heading: The regional floor is much lower than the national average
sections.body: BARMM had the lowest traditional contraceptive use at 3.2 percent, less than half the national level of 8.1 percent. Cagayan Valley at 4.1 percent, Central Visayas at 4.8 percent, NIR at 5.2 percent, MIMAROPA at 5.5 percent, and Zamboanga Peninsula¹ at 5.6 percent also sat well below the national figure. These regions are not low because one traditional method disappears; both withdrawal and calendar or rhythm are generally limited in the lower-use group. The gap between Eastern Visayas and BARMM was 8.9 percentage points, which is larger than the national traditional-use level itself.

sections.heading: The biggest regional changes were declines, not gains
sections.body: Most regions moved downward between 2022 and 2025, but the size of the decline varied sharply. BARMM fell from 8.9 percent to 3.2 percent, Central Visayas from 10.2 percent to 4.8 percent, and Bicol Region from 14.3 percent to 9.7 percent. MIMAROPA also dropped from 8.7 percent to 5.5 percent. Only a few regions increased, and the largest rise was Caraga, from 6.5 percent to 7.4 percent.

sections.heading: Traditional use is concentrated in the middle and later reproductive ages
sections.body: Traditional contraceptive use was very low among women aged 15 to 19, at 0.6 percent, and rose to 4.5 percent among women aged 20 to 24. It reached 11.5 percent at ages 30 to 34 and 12.7 percent at both ages 35 to 39 and 40 to 44. The age pattern then eased to 10.0 percent at ages 45 to 49. Across these age groups, withdrawal remained the larger traditional method, while calendar or rhythm became more visible at older ages, reaching 3.0 percent at ages 45 to 49.

sections.heading: Women with one or two children had the highest traditional-use level
sections.body: By number of living children, traditional use was highest among women with one or two children, at 14.7 percent in 2025. It was lower among women with three or four children at 11.0 percent and among women with five or more children at 10.9 percent. Women with no living children had much lower traditional use at 1.8 percent, which reflects the all-women denominator as well as family-building stage. The sharpest declines from 2022 were among women with five or more children, down 4.4 percentage points, and women with three or four children, down 3.5 points.

sections.heading: Urban and rural women looked similar compared with the regional spread
sections.body: Traditional contraceptive use was 8.6 percent among urban women and 7.5 percent among rural women in 2025. Both figures declined from 2022, when urban women were at 10.2 percent and rural women were at 9.4 percent. Withdrawal again explains most of the level in both places, at 7.1 percent in urban areas and 6.3 percent in rural areas. The 1.1-point urban-rural gap is small beside the much wider differences across regions.

sections.heading: Education does not form a simple ladder
sections.body: Traditional use was highest among women with post-secondary education, at 13.9 percent in 2025, while college graduates were lower at 9.0 percent. Women with Grades 7 to 10 were at 8.5 percent, Grades 1 to 6 at 7.8 percent, and no education at 4.3 percent. Grades 11 to 12 stood apart at 3.4 percent, much lower than adjacent education groups. The biggest education shift from 2022 was among post-secondary women, where traditional use rose from 9.4 percent to 13.9 percent, driven by withdrawal rising from 6.6 percent to 12.1 percent.

sections.heading: Wealth differences were modest, with the middle quintile highest
sections.body: Traditional contraceptive use did not rise or fall steadily with wealth in 2025. The middle wealth quintile had the highest level at 9.2 percent, followed by the highest quintile at 8.4 percent and the second quintile at 8.0 percent. The lowest quintile was at 7.0 percent and the fourth quintile at 7.6 percent. Compared with 2022, the largest declines were in the lowest quintile, down from 10.1 percent to 7.0 percent, and the fourth quintile, down from 10.4 percent to 7.6 percent.

takeaways: Traditional contraceptive use among all women declined to 8.1 percent in 2025, with withdrawal accounting for most of both the level and the drop from 2022.
Regional differences are the clearest divide: Eastern Visayas and Ilocos Region were near 12 percent, while BARMM was 3.2 percent.
The background patterns are uneven rather than ladder-like, with use highest among women aged 35 to 44, women with one or two children, post-secondary women, and the middle wealth quintile.
footnotes: ¹ Includes the province of Sulu in 2025.
